Product.title: Product
Product.description: Represents a product in the Bikir-Climat store.

description.description: A detailed description of the product.

pricePmr.description: The price of the product in PMR (Rubles).

priceMd.description: The price of the product in MD (Lei).

images.description: An array of image URLs for the product.

specs.description: Object that contains various product specifications such as area, type, and inverter. Stored as a string.

category.description: The category of the product (e.g., cond, service).

stockStatus.description: Indicates whether the product is in stock.

Order.title: Order
Order.description: Represents an order placed by a customer.

customerName.description: The name of the customer who placed the order.

items.description: An array of product IDs in the order.

totalPrice.description: The total price of the order.

currency.description: The currency used for the order (PMR or MD).

status.description: The status of the order (e.g., new, done).

definition.description: Stores product information.  Admin users have read/write access.
Stores order information. Accessible to admins. Users can create orders.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore data structure is designed to manage products and orders for the Bikir-Climat webstore. It prioritizes structural segregation and clarity. Products are stored in a top-level `products` collection, and orders are stored in a top-level `orders` collection. This approach avoids mixing data with different access needs within the same collection.

This structure simplifies security rules by ensuring that all documents within a collection share the same security requirements. Products can be publicly accessible (for read operations), while write operations are restricted to authenticated admins (PIN code authentication is emulated on the client-side, so security rules still need to protect the data). Orders will be readable/writable by authenticated admins and writable by authenticated users (once user authentication is implemented).

This structure enables secure `list` operations by ensuring that the security rules don't act as filters. All documents in a collection have the same access requirements, so listing the entire collection is secure based on the role of the user making the request.
